tweets.text: I think: inflation pressure is overstated; labor market inflecting higher not overheating; private credit risk eh; nonbank leverage is whatever; the Fed isn’t doing YCC or need to backstop the Treasury mkt yet; 10y has just about peaked and 4.5% seems fair; we’ll be alright. 🤷🏻‍♂️
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
Warsh may be walking into one of the hardest Fed setups in modern history.

Inflation pressure. Labor market uncertainty. Private credit risk. Nonbank leverage. A Treasury market that may matter more than any formal mandate.

I discussed it with Danielle DiMartino Booth, https://t.co/xOr3FSLlw2
tweets.full_text: I think: inflation pressure is overstated; labor market inflecting higher not overheating; private credit risk eh; nonbank leverage is whatever; the Fed isn’t doing YCC or need to backstop the Treasury mkt yet; 10y has just about peaked and 4.5% seems fair; we’ll be alright. 🤷🏻‍♂️

tweets.text:
Protocols on Solana are pulling in decent revenue even in the current market.

Here are the top 10 by 24h revenue:

1. @Pumpfun
2. @Collector_Crypt
3. @AxiomExchange
4. @zinc_cash
5. @phantom
6. @JupiterExchange
7. @ORE
8. @dexscreener
9. @fomo
10. @MeteoraAG

ZINC really stands out here, as it's only a 2-week-old protocol.

From what I found, it’s a proof-of-work mining protocol that runs on @Arcium’s confidential compute.

It already has 325K SOL ($20M) in round volume since launch, with roughly 10% ($2M) of that converting to protocol revenue.

Always interesting to watch new and unique concepts develop in this space.

tweets.text:
100%, we don’t have an idea crisis anymore.

Everything you might think of is likely already released or in development.

Attention is getting ultra scarce.

I guess from now on, VCs will start investing in distribution, not ideas or prototypes.

tweets.text:
i'm a vision pro bull but i think this was the most bearish wwdc for vision pro since launch and i don't understand how you guys manage to not see that

visionos was a second class citizen! amazingly it doesn't get access to apple's most powerful local model (the one that only iphone 17 pro, M4+ ipads, and M3+ Macs get) even though the newer vision pro with M5 has the specs for it. which means it won't have access to the improved voice dictation, even though it's the one device that needs it most!

and there's really very few platform-specific features for it. feels like a bunch of leftovers that weren't ready for past releases.

My spelling has gone from pretty great 20 years ago to god awful today because of relying on auto correct

Not sure what other AI specific ones there are

Gym couple times a week, rock climbing once a week, golf twice a week

tweets.text: Make sure you're following the suggestions made earlier, like building meaningful relationships and constantly getting in sync. tweets.text:
Have you ever considered the vast majority of CT holds me to an impossible standard? 

When I publish an investigation about manipulated tokens before insiders exit you accuse me of shorting (RAVE,  M, LAB)

When I publish about an investigation after you claim I won’t say anything publicly until the damage is done.

Followers such as yourself have discouraged me from posting my investigations regularly since it’s not worth the stress when I can continue my work in private.

Instead of doing the work yourself you defer it all to me.

tweets.text:
We are entering the age of just-in-time software. Some of my coworkers are visiting San Francisco this week, so I asked Claude Fable to design an extremely detailed HTML map of San Francisco so I can explain the city to them.

The HTML is wild. It has all the street names, their exact dimensions, the relief of the city, the fog, the sun exposure etc.

Everything it real. I was super surprised but it went and found the city's actual data, then built a cartographic engine around it. So I have: 

- Every street from the SF Public Works centerline survey: 15,905 segments, 2,502 named streets, 54,989 surveyed vertices, with the official address ranges per block
- The relief from 14,151 municipal elevation contour points, interpolated into a 168,000-vertex terrain model
- 9,672 buildings with lidar-measured heights from an SF Planning study
- Every Muni Metro line, the cable cars and BART from the official GTFS feed: real route shapes, real 10-minute headways, real speeds. 76 little trams move on the map at their scheduled pace, and the K dims when it passes under 175 meters of Twin Peaks because it knows the tunnel portals (wtf!)
- Fog modeled as marine-layer advection over the terrain, calibrated on NREL satellite irradiance and a decade of ASOS weather observations.

Claude did totally overkilled stuff like the fog model's 50% line landed at longitude -122.437 which is... Divisadero Street (every San Franciscan knows the fog stops there)

The whole thing is one self-contained HTML file, 1.2 MB, with a WebGL engine. No libraries, no API calls, no network. It runs offline forever.

We talk about scaling and benchmarks all day. Sometimes the bitter lesson is simpler: give the machine real data and real verification loops, and it builds you the city.

I'm spending the evening exploring the map; it's too good!
tweets.full_text:
We are entering the age of just-in-time software. Some of my coworkers are visiting San Francisco this week, so I asked Claude Fable to design an extremely detailed HTML map of San Francisco so I can explain the city to them.

The HTML is wild. It has all the street names, their exact dimensions, the relief of the city, the fog, the sun exposure etc.

Everything it real. I was super surprised but it went and found the city's actual data, then built a cartographic engine around it. So I have: 

- Every street from the SF Public Works centerline survey: 15,905 segments, 2,502 named streets, 54,989 surveyed vertices, with the official address ranges per block
- The relief from 14,151 municipal elevation contour points, interpolated into a 168,000-vertex terrain model
- 9,672 buildings with lidar-measured heights from an SF Planning study
- Every Muni Metro line, the cable cars and BART from the official GTFS feed: real route shapes, real 10-minute headways, real speeds. 76 little trams move on the map at their scheduled pace, and the K dims when it passes under 175 meters of Twin Peaks because it knows the tunnel portals (wtf!)
- Fog modeled as marine-layer advection over the terrain, calibrated on NREL satellite irradiance and a decade of ASOS weather observations.

Claude did totally overkilled stuff like the fog model's 50% line landed at longitude -122.437 which is... Divisadero Street (every San Franciscan knows the fog stops there)

The whole thing is one self-contained HTML file, 1.2 MB, with a WebGL engine. No libraries, no API calls, no network. It runs offline forever.

We talk about scaling and benchmarks all day. Sometimes the bitter lesson is simpler: give the machine real data and real verification loops, and it builds you the city.

I'm spending the evening exploring the map; it's too good!
